description_blurb:
Apollo 11 was the third crewed mission to the Moon and the first to land Astronauts on the lunar surface. This document is an excerpt from the Apollo 11 Technical Crew Debriefing (Volumes 1 and 2) from July 31, 1969. The document highlights three observations: one, an object on the way out to the Moon; two, flashes of light inside the cabin; and three, a sighting on the return trip of a bright light tentatively assumed by the crew to be a laser.

•	Page 6-33 (Vol. 1). [Lunar Module Pilot for Apollo 11, Buzz Aldrin]: “The first unusual thing that we saw I guess was 1 day out or something pretty close to the moon. It had a sizeable dimension to it, so we put the monocular on it.” The crew speculated that it could have been the S-IVB stage of the Saturn V launch vehicle.
•	Page 6-37 (Vol. 1). [Lunar Module Pilot for Apollo 11, Buzz Aldrin] “The other observation that I made accumulated gradually. I don’t know whether I saw it the first night, but I’m sure I saw it the second night. I was trying to go to sleep with all the lights out. I observed what I thought were little flashes inside the cabin, spaced a couple of minutes apart…”
•	Page 21-1 (Vol. 2). [Lunar Module Pilot for Apollo 11, Buzz Aldrin] “I observed what appeared to be a fairly bright light source which we tentatively ascribed to a possible laser.”
title: NASA-UAP-D4, Apollo 11 Technical Crew Debriefing, 1969
summary: This document is an excerpt from the Apollo 11 Technical Crew Debriefing (Volumes 1 and 2) from July 31, 1969, detailing three unusual observations made by the crew during the mission. First, a sizeable object was observed on the way to the Moon, described with various shapes including L-shaped, open-suitcase-like, two connected rings, or a hollow cylinder, which the crew speculated could be the S-IVB stage or debris from the spacecraft. Second, flashes of light were seen inside the cabin, possibly caused by atomic particle penetration. Third, a fairly bright light source was sighted from lunar orbit and on the return trip, initially thought to be a laser, but later concluded to be a reflection of the Sun from a body of water on Earth.
reported_object_description: First object: sizeable dimension, L shape, open suitcase, two connected rings, hollow cylinder, open-book shape. Second observation: little flashes, double flashes, line with no direction of motion. Third observation: fairly bright light source.
reported_behavior: First object: going by, tumbling. Second observation: spaced a couple of minutes apart, continued to be there, entry and then impact, zapping through the cabin. Third observation: coming from the Earth.
official_resolution: The first object's identity remained inconclusive, with speculation ranging from the S-IVB stage to Mylar or a part of the high gain antenna. The flashes were attributed to the penetration of atomic particles. The bright light source was concluded to be a reflection of the Sun from a smooth body of water on Earth, revising an initial assumption of a laser.
bust_reasoning: The document itself provides strong mundane explanations for all three observations. The Apollo 11 crew actively debriefed and concluded that the first object was likely spacecraft debris, the second observation was consistent with atomic particle interactions (cosmic ray phosphenes), and the third was sunlight reflecting off water. These are either explicitly identified prosaic sources or highly plausible mundane explanations consistent with the crew's own theories.
full_text:
# NASA-UAP-D4, Apollo 11 Technical Crew Debriefing, 1969 - Page 1 94 251 pgs ~~CONFIDENTIAL~~ NATIONAL AERONAUTICS AND SPACE ADMINISTRATION APOLLO 11 TECHNICAL CREW DEBRIEFING (U) JULY 31, 1969 [Left Margin - Handwritten/Stamp] CLASSIFICATION CHANGED TO BY AUTHORITY OF E.O. 11652 6/1/72 5-11-72 Sely D. [illegible] PREPARED BY: MISSION OPERATIONS BRANCH FLIGHT CREW SUPPORT DIVISION VOL. I +-------------------------------------------------------+ | GROUP 4 | | Downgraded at 3-year | | intervals; declassified | | after 12 years | +-------------------------------------------------------+ This material within the me transmission prohibited by NOTICE: This document may be exempt from public disclosure under the Freedom of Infor- mation Act (5 U.S.C. 552). Requests for its re- lease to persons outside the U.S. Government should be handled under the provisions of NASA Policy Directive 1382.2. United States 3 and 794, the person is CENTER ~~CONFIDENTIAL~~ INDEXING DATA | DATE | OPR | # | T | PGM | SUBJECT | SIGNATOR | LOC | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 07-31-69 | MSC | | R | APO | (Title) | MSC | 078-62 | # NASA-UAP-D4, Apollo 11 Technical Crew Debriefing, 1969 - Page 2
CONFIDENTIAL
6-33
| | | | :--- | :--- | | COLLINS | I think particularly when you get into the later flights of extended EVA's and lunar activity, somehow the crew must place themselves in a frame of mind of looking on the separation of the LM as the beginning of the flight plan and to relax, get plenty of sleep, and conserve their energies in all the events leading up to that point. To arrive in lunar orbit tired can create problems and it's possible to do that if you don't approach it in the right frame of mind. | | ARMSTRONG | I think Mike's hit the nail on the head. We did precisely that. We got a lot of rest and got into lunar orbit eager to go to work and that's a particularly fortunate position to be in. | | COLLINS | This is something we've talked about before the flight and I don't # NASA-UAP-D4, Apollo 11 Technical Crew Debriefing, 1969 - Page 3 6-34 ~~CONFIDENTIAL~~ COLLINS: How'd we see this thing? Did we just look out the window and there it was? ALDRIN: Yes, and we weren't sure but what it might be the S-IVB. We called the ground and were told the S-IVB was 6000 miles away. We had a problem with the high gain about this time, didn't we? COLLINS: There was something. We felt a bump or maybe I just imagined it. ARMSTRONG: He was wondering whether the MESA had come off. COLLINS: I don't guess we felt anything. ALDRIN: Of course, we were seeing all sorts of little objects going by at the various dumps and then we happened to see this one brighter object going by. We couldn't think of anything else it could be other than the S-IVB. We looked at it through the monocular and it seemed to have a bit of an L shape to it. ARMSTRONG: Like an open suitcase. ALDRIN: We were in PTC at the time so each one of us had a chance to take a look at this and it certainly seemed to be with- in our vicinity and of a very sizeable dimension. ~~CONFIDENTIAL~~ # NASA-UAP-D4, Apollo 11 Technical Crew Debriefing, 1969 - Page 4 ~~CONFIDENTIAL~~ | 6-35 | | :--- | :--- | | ARMSTRONG | We should say that it was right at the limit of the resolution of the eye. It was very difficult to tell just what shape it was. And there was no way to tell the size without knowing the range or the range without knowing the size. | | ALDRIN | So then I got down in the LEB and started looking for it in the optics. We were grossly mislead because with the sextant off focus what we saw appeared to be a cylinder. | | ARMSTRONG | Or really two rings. | | ALDRIN | Yes. | | ARMSTRONG | Two rings. Two connected rings. | | COLLINS | No, it looked like a hollow cylinder to me. It didn't look like two connected rings. You could see this thing tumbling and, when it came around end-on, you could look right down in its guts. It was a hollow cylinder. But then you could change the focus on the sextant and it would be replaced by this open-book shape. It was really weird. | | ALDRIN | I guess there's not too much more to say about it other than it wasn't a cylinder. | | | ~~CONFIDENTIAL~~ | # NASA-UAP-D4, Apollo 11 Technical Crew Debriefing, 1969 - Page 5 6-36 ~~CONFIDENTIAL~~ COLLINS: It was during the period when we thought it was a cylinder that we inquired about the S-IVB and we'd almost convinced ourselves that's what it had to be. But we don't have any more conclusions than that really. The fact that we didn't see it much past this one time period — we really don't have a conclusion as to what it might have been, how big it was, or how far away it was. It was something that wasn't part of the urine dump, we're pretty sure of that. Skipping ahead a bit, when we jettisoned the LM, you know we fired an explosive charge and got rid of the docking rings and the LM went boom. Pieces came off the LM. It could have been some Mylar or something that had somehow come loose from the LM. ALDRIN: We thought it could have been a panel, but it didn't appear to have that shape at all. COLLINS: That's right, and for some reason, we thought it might have been a part of the high gain antenna. It might have been about the time we had high gain antenna problems. In the back of my mind, I have some reason to suspect that its origin was from the spacecraft. ~~CONFIDENTIAL~~ # NASA-UAP-D4, Apollo 11 Technical Crew Debriefing, 1969 - Page 6 ~~CONFIDENTIAL~~ 6-37 ALDRIN The other observation that I made accumulated gradually. I don't know whether I saw it the first night, but I'm sure I saw it the second night. I was trying to go to sleep with all the lights out. I observed what I thought were little flashes inside the cabin, spaced a couple of minutes apart and I didn't think too much about it other than just note in my mind that they continued to be there. I couldn't explain why my eye would see these flashes. During transearth coast, we had more time and I devoted more opportunity to investigating what this could have been. It was at that point that I was able to observe on two different occasions that, instead of observing just one flash, I could see double flashes, at points separated by maybe a foot. At other times, I could see a line with no direction of motion and the only thing that comes to my mind is that this is some sort of pene- tration. At least that's my guess, without much to sup- port it; some penetration of some object into the spacecraft that causes an emission as it enters the cabin itself. Sometimes it was one flash on entering. Possibly departing from an entirely different part of the cabin, outside the field of view. The double flashes appeared to have an entry and then impact on something such as the struts. For a while, I thought it might have been ~~CONFIDENTIAL~~ # NASA-UAP-D4, Apollo 11 Technical Crew Debriefing, 1969 - Page 7 6-38 ~~CONFIDENTIAL~~ ALDRIN (CONT'D) some static electricity because I was also able, in moving my hand up and down the sleep restraint, to generate very small sparks of static electricity. But there was a definite difference between the two as I observed it more and more. I tried to correlate this with the direction of the sun. When you put the window shades up there is still a small amount of leakage. You can generally tell within 20 or 30 degrees the direction of the sun. It seemed as though they were coming from that general direction; however, I really couldn't say if there was near enough evidence to support that these things were observable on the side of the spacecraft where the sun was. A little bit of evidence seemed to support this. I asked the others if they had seen any of these and, until about the last day, they hadn't. ARMSTRONG Buzz, I'd seen some light, but I just always attributed this to sunlight, because the window covers leak a little bit of light no matter how tightly secured. The only time I observed it was the last night when we really looked for it. I spent probably an hour carefully watching the inside of the spacecraft and I probably made 50 significant observations in this period. ~~CONFIDENTIAL~~ # NASA-UAP-D4, Apollo 11 Technical Crew Debriefing, 1969 - Page 8 ~~CONFIDENTIAL~~ 6-39 | | | | :--- | :--- | | ALDRIN | Sometimes a minute or two would go by and then you'd see the two within the space of 10 seconds. On an average, I'd say just as a guess it was maybe something like one a minute. Certainly more than enough to convince you that it wasn't an optical illusion. It did give you a rather funny feeling to contemplate that something was zapping through the cabin. There wasn't anything you could do about it. | | ARMSTRONG | It could be something like Buzz suggested. Mainly a neutron or some kind of an atomic particle that would be in the visible spectrum. | ~~CONFIDENTIAL~~ # NASA-UAP-D4, Apollo 11 Technical Crew Debriefing, 1969 - Page 9 216 pgs 94 ~~CONFIDENTIAL~~ [NASA Logo] NATIONAL AERONAUTICS AND SPACE ADMINISTRATION APOLLO 11 TECHNICAL CREW DEBRIEFING (U) [Handwritten/Stamped marginalia (rotated 90 degrees)]: CLASSIFICATION CHANGED TO U BY AUTHORITY OF E.O. 11652 6/1/72 DATE 3/14/76 [Signature/Name] JULY 31, 1969 PREPARED BY: MISSION OPERATIONS BRANCH FLIGHT CREW SUPPORT DIVISION VOL. II GROUP 4 Downgraded at 3-year intervals; declassified after 12 years This material c... [illegible] within the mean [illegible] transmission or [illegible] prohibited by la [illegible] NOTICE: This document may be exempt from public disclosure under the Freedom of Information Act (5 U.S.C. 552). Requests for its release to persons outside the U.S. Government should be handled under the provisions of NASA Policy Directive 1382.2. CENTER ~~CONFIDENTIAL~~ | DATE | OPR | # | T | PGM | SUBJECT | SIGNATOR | LOC | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 07-31-69 | MSC | | | APO | (Title) | MSC | 078-62 | [Handwritten note below table]: DUP? # NASA-UAP-D4, Apollo 11 Technical Crew Debriefing, 1969 - Page 10 ~~CONFIDENTIAL~~ 21-1 21.0 VISUAL SIGHTINGS ARMSTRONG: Most of the items in Section 21, Visual Sightings, have been previously reported. 21.4 TRANSLUNAR AND TRANSEARTH FLIGHT ALDRIN: There was only one minor observation returning from the Moon. Looking back at it, at a time after Mars had passed behind the Moon, there was one time period where I imagined that the image of Mars was coming from a region where it couldn't come from, because it was in a dark portion of the Moon. This obviously was an optical illusion of some sort. ARMSTRONG: I suspect that it was, in fact, just immediately adjacent to the horizon. ALDRIN: We must have looked at it immediately after it had come from the back side. ARMSTRONG: Yes. 21.5 LUNAR ORBIT ALDRIN: In lunar orbit, following ascent, we did note and mention to the ground that approaching CDH when the Earth came up above the lunar horizon, I observed what appeared to be a fairly bright light source which we tentatively ascribed ~~CONFIDENTIAL~~ # NASA-UAP-D4, Apollo 11 Technical Crew Debriefing, 1969 - Page 11 21-2
~~CONFIDENTIAL~~
ALDRIN (CONT'D) to a possible laser. That seemed to be the best possible explanation until we were coming back in the command module approaching the Earth and were able to observe something that gave about the same appearance. When putting the monocular on the light source, it appeared as though it was the reflection of the Sun from a relatively smooth body of water such as a lake. I think we've revised our initial conclusion as to what the source of that light was that we saw coming from the Earth. If no one owns up to having beamed the laser toward the Moon at that time, it was more probably a reflection off a lake. I still think it's an unusual phenomenon, at that distance, to see so bright a source of light. In the film, it didn't appear as though this was going to show up at all. The Earth was too bright.
~~CONFIDENTIAL~~
cover_up_reasoning: The document was initially classified 'CONFIDENTIAL' but was explicitly declassified to 'UNCLASSIFIED' years later, as indicated by the stamps on multiple pages. There are no content redactions, references to classified annexes, or indications of suppressed information, only standard FOIA boilerplate.
title_zh: 美国国家航空航天局UAP-D4，阿波罗11号技术乘员汇报，1969年
summary_zh: 本文件摘自1969年7月31日的《阿波罗11号技术乘员汇报》（第一卷和第二卷），详细记录了乘员在任务期间的三项异常观测。首先，在前往月球途中观测到一个尺寸可观的物体，形态各异，包括L形、类似打开的行李箱、两个相连的环或中空圆柱体，乘员推测其可能为S-IVB级火箭或航天器碎片。其次，在舱内观测到闪光，可能由原子粒子穿透引起。第三，在月球轨道和返程途中观测到一个相当明亮的光源，最初被认为是激光，但后来认定为地球水体对太阳光的反射。
reported_object_description_zh: 第一个物体：尺寸可观，L形，打开的行李箱状，两个相连的环，中空圆柱体，书本打开状。第二次观测：微弱闪光，双重闪光，无运动方向的线条。第三次观测：相当明亮的光源。
reported_behavior_zh: 第一个物体：经过，翻滚。第二次观测：相隔数分钟，持续存在，进入后撞击，穿过舱内。第三次观测：来自地球。
official_resolution_zh: 第一个物体的身份仍无定论，推测范围从S-IVB级火箭到聚酯薄膜（Mylar）或高增益天线的一部分。闪光被归因于原子粒子的穿透。明亮光源被认定为地球上平滑水体对太阳光的反射，修正了最初关于激光的假设。
bust_reasoning_zh: 文件本身为所有三项观测提供了有力的世俗解释。阿波罗11号乘员积极汇报并得出结论：第一个物体可能是航天器碎片；第二次观测与原子粒子相互作用（宇宙射线闪光幻影）相符；第三次观测是太阳光从水面反射。这些要么是明确识别的普通来源，要么是与乘员自身理论相符的高度合理的世俗解释。
cover_up_reasoning_zh: 该文件最初被列为“机密”，但多年后被明确解密为“非机密”，如多页上的印章所示。文件中没有内容删节、没有提及机密附件，也没有压制信息的迹象，仅有标准的《信息自由法》样板声明。
claims: Crew observed a sizeable object on the way to the Moon, initially thought to be the S-IVB stage or spacecraft debris.
Crew observed flashes of light inside the cabin, possibly caused by atomic particle penetration.
Crew observed a bright light source from lunar orbit/return trip, initially thought to be a laser, later believed to be sun reflection off water.
claims_zh: 乘员在前往月球途中观测到一个尺寸可观的物体，最初被认为是S-IVB级火箭或航天器碎片。
乘员在舱内观测到闪光，可能由原子粒子穿透引起。
乘员在月球轨道/返程途中观测到一个明亮光源，最初被认为是激光，后来被认为是太阳光从水面反射。
bust_explanations: The 'first object' was likely debris from the Apollo 11 spacecraft itself (e.g., Mylar, a panel, or part of the high-gain antenna), as considered by the crew, who noted its indistinct shape and inability to determine size or range.

The 'second observation' of flashes and lines inside the cabin is highly consistent with cosmic ray visual phenomena (phosphenes), a known effect of high-energy particles interacting with astronauts' retinas or brains, which Armstrong explicitly suggested as 'a neutron or some kind of an atomic particle'.
The 'third observation' of a bright light source from Earth was concluded by the crew to be a reflection of the Sun from a smooth body of water, such as a lake, after initially considering a laser.
bust_explanations_zh: “第一个物体”很可能是阿波罗11号航天器本身的碎片（例如，聚酯薄膜、一块面板或高增益天线的一部分），乘员曾如此考虑，他们注意到其形状模糊且无法确定尺寸或距离。
“第二次观测”到的舱内闪光和线条与宇宙射线视觉现象（闪光幻影）高度一致，这是高能粒子与宇航员视网膜或大脑相互作用的已知效应，阿姆斯特朗明确提出其可能为“中子或某种原子粒子”。
乘员得出结论，“第三次观测”到的来自地球的明亮光源是太阳光从平滑水体（例如湖泊）的反射，此前他们曾初步考虑为激光。
